title: Multi-Modal Observations
concept: Integrating pixel screenshots alongside text logs to give visual grounding for UI tasks.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why does an agent use a standardized observation schema for tool feedback?

options.text: So successes, warnings, and crashes are always formatted the same way and easy to parse
So the agent can run without any tools at all
So the screen looks prettier for humans

exercises.explain: A shared schema means the agent reads every result the same way, so it never gets confused by messy or inconsistent feedback formats.

cards.title: Text Isn't Always Enough
cards.body: Imagine describing a website using only words: 'blue button, top-right.' An agent can miss it. Sometimes it needs to actually SEE the screen.

cards.title: Multi-Modal Observations
cards.body: This means the agent looks at more than one type of input at once. It reads text logs AND views pixel screenshots of the screen together.

cards.title: Directions Plus a Photo
cards.body: Text logs are like written directions: 'turn left at the store.' A screenshot is like a photo of that store. Together, you find your way much faster.

cards.title: Clicking Checkout
cards.body: The log says 'button labeled Checkout exists.' The screenshot shows it's greyed out and disabled. Now the agent knows not to click yet.

cards.title: Myth: Screenshots Replace Text
cards.body: Not true! Pixels show layout, but text logs give exact labels and errors. The best agents use both together for full grounding.

cards.title: Two Views, One Task
cards.body: Text tells the agent WHAT is there; the screenshot shows WHERE and HOW it looks.

left.text: 'Submit button loaded' — exact words, but no position or color.

right.text: Shows the button is at the bottom and hidden behind a popup.

exercises.prompt: Why would an agent doing a UI task want a screenshot along with text logs?

options.text: To visually confirm where things are and how they look, grounding the text in reality
To make the task take much longer for no reason
To avoid ever reading any text again

exercises.explain: A screenshot gives visual grounding: the agent can see position, layout, and state (like disabled buttons) that plain text might not capture.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Combining pixel screenshots with text logs gives the agent visual grounding for UI tasks.
exercises.explain: True! Seeing the actual screen alongside the text description helps the agent understand where elements are and whether they're clickable.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about multi-modal observation.
exercises.sentence: Adding a screenshot to text logs gives the agent visual grounding for UI tasks.
exercises.explain: Visual grounding means the agent ties its text understanding to what it actually sees on screen, so it acts on reality, not assumptions.

exercises.prompt: Which observation was written by an AI agent combining a screenshot with its text log?

options.text: Log says 'Save button present'; screenshot shows it greyed out, so I'll wait until the form is complete before clicking.
I clicked the button because someone told me to, no idea what it looked like.
The screen is nice today, I feel good about it.

exercises.explain: The AI-style observation cross-checks the text log against the pixels it sees, then makes a grounded decision — that's multi-modal reasoning.

exercises.prompt: An agent's log reads 'Confirm button loaded.' But when it tries to click, nothing happens. Its screenshot shows a popup covering the button. What is the best multi-modal move?

options.text: Use the screenshot to see the popup, close it first, then click Confirm — combining both views to act correctly
Trust only the text log and click the same spot forever
Ignore the screenshot because text is always right
Delete the log and give up on the task

exercises.explain: The text log alone said the button was ready, but the screenshot revealed a popup blocking it. By integrating pixels with text, the agent sees the real obstacle, clears it, then acts. That's the power of multi-modal observation: visual grounding fills the gaps text can't show.
